channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: On-chip Parametric Amplification in a Double Quantum Dots Circuit
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates a clever solution to a persistent bottleneck in quantum computing: achieving high-fidelity qubit readouts without the drawbacks of traditional parametric amplifiers. By embedding amplification directly within double quantum dots themselves, the researchers created an "on-chip" amplifier that eliminates insertion losses and magnetic field vulnerabilities that plague conventional external amplifiers. The 11 dB gain and doubling of signal-to-noise ratio represents a significant practical advance for reading quantum states accurately, which is crucial for error correction and reliable quantum operations. This approach could streamline quantum processor architectures by integrating amplification functionality directly into the quantum circuit elements, potentially making quantum systems more compact and robust.

In microwave-based quantum circuits, including double quantum dots (DQDs), superconducting qubits and spin qubits, parametric amplifiers are indispensable in achieving high-fidelity qubit readouts. Despite its importance, the application of parametric amplifiers is hampered by several challenges, such as high insertion losses, constrained tunability, and a pronounced vulnerability to magnetic fields. Here, we demonstrate an on-site single-atom parametric amplifier (SAPA) within a reconfigurable quantum circuit, which consists of a superconducting microwave cavity and two GaAs gate-defined DQDs. Leveraging the inherent nonlinearity of the DQD, a parametric gain exceeding 11 dB is achieved. This gain contributes to enhance the qubit readout, as evidenced by exceeding two times improvement in the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) when employing the DQD-based amplifier for reading out another DQD. Our work not only presents a versatile experimental platform with enhanced readout capabilities in quantum computing, but also introduces alternative choices of parametric amplifiers for a variety of microwave-based quantum circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Barren Plateaus Beyond Observable Concentration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research provides crucial insight into one of quantum machine learning's biggest roadblocks by revealing that the dreaded "barren plateau" problem actually stems from multiple distinct mechanisms rather than a single cause. The authors demonstrate that while avoiding observable concentration is necessary for trainable quantum circuits, it's not sufficient - circuits can still become untrainable due to information loss or scrambling within the quantum computation itself. This unified framework could be a game-changer for designing more effective parameterized quantum circuits, as it gives researchers specific targets to address rather than treating barren plateaus as a monolithic problem. The work is particularly timely as the quantum machine learning community grapples with scaling up quantum algorithms beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations.

Parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) are central to quantum machine learning and near-term quantum simulation, but their scalability is often hindered by barren plateaus (BPs), where gradients decay exponentially with system size. Prior explanations, including expressivity, entanglement, locality, and noise, are often presented in ways that conflate two distinct issues: concentration of the measured observable and loss of parameter sensitivity caused by circuit dynamics. We develop a unified statistical framework that separates these mechanisms. We show that several standard BP explanations, including locality- and entanglement-related effects, can be understood through a single phenomenon that we term observable concentration (OC). Importantly, we prove that avoiding OC is necessary but not sufficient for trainability. Beyond OC, we identify two distinct mid-circuit sources of gradient suppression. First, mid-circuit information loss occurs when parameter perturbations propagate into degrees of freedom that are inaccessible to the final measurement, yielding little or no response. Second, mid-circuit information scrambling occurs when local perturbations rapidly spread across the system and become effectively undetectable on the measured subsystem. We support our theory with explicit constructions and numerical evidence, including quantum convolutional neural network architectures that exhibit information-loss-induced barren plateaus despite the absence of observable concentration.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Simulating Quantum Error Correction beyond Pauli Stochastic Errors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing blind spots in quantum error correction: most fault-tolerance analyses assume well-behaved Pauli errors, but real quantum hardware produces messy coherent and non-Pauli errors that could dramatically undermine error correction performance. The authors developed new simulation techniques to study these realistic error types and found some concerning results - coherent errors can shift fault-tolerance thresholds and increase logical error rates by an order of magnitude compared to the idealized Pauli errors typically studied. This work provides crucial tools for understanding how quantum error correction will actually perform on real hardware, potentially requiring significant adjustments to current fault-tolerance strategies as we move toward practical quantum computing systems. The ability to map complex physical noise models onto tractable detector error models represents a major advance in bridging the gap between theoretical quantum error correction and real-world implementation challenges.

Quantum error correction (QEC), the lynchpin of fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC), is designed and validated against well-behaved Pauli stochastic error models. But in real-world deployment, QEC protocols encounter a vast array of other errors -- coherent and non-Pauli errors -- whose impacts on quantum circuits are vastly different than those of stochastic Pauli errors. The impacts of these errors on QEC and FTQC protocols have been largely unpredictable to date due to exponential classical simulation cost. Here, we show how to accurately and efficiently model the effects of coherent and non-Pauli errors on FTQC, and we study the effects of such errors on syndrome extraction for surface and bivariate bicycle codes, and on magic state cultivation. Our analysis suggests that coherent error can shift fault-tolerance thresholds, increase the space-time cost of magic state cultivation, and can increase logical error rates by an order of magnitude compared to equivalent stochastic errors. These analyses are enabled by a new technique for mapping any Markovian circuit-level error model with sufficiently small error rates onto a detector error model (DEM) for an FTQC circuit. The resulting DEM enables Monte Carlo estimation of logical error rates and noise-adapted decoding, and its parameters can be analytically related to the underlying physical noise parameters to enable approximate strong simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award
item.description:
Curator's Take: This recognition marks a historic moment as Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard become the first quantum physicists to receive the Turing Award, computing's most prestigious honor, for their groundbreaking work on quantum cryptography and the BB84 protocol. Their 1984 theoretical framework for quantum key distribution didn't just launch the field of quantum cryptography - it provided the mathematical foundation that decades later would enable today's quantum-secured communications networks and inform the development of quantum-resistant encryption standards. The award signals the computing community's acknowledgment that quantum information science has matured from exotic physics into a fundamental pillar of future computing infrastructure. This milestone also underscores how foundational theoretical work in quantum mechanics continues to drive practical innovations that will reshape cybersecurity in our increasingly digital world.

Duo bag award often described as the “Nobel Prize in Computing” The post Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem
item.description:
Curator's Take: Denmark's quantum ecosystem is rapidly emerging as a major European hub, attracting top international talent through strategic investments and collaborative research initiatives. This development signals a significant shift in the global quantum landscape, where smaller nations with focused strategies can compete effectively against larger quantum programs by leveraging their strengths in fundamental research and fostering tight-knit innovation networks. The influx of international scientists suggests Denmark is successfully creating the kind of collaborative environment that has proven essential for quantum breakthroughs, positioning itself as a key player in Europe's broader quantum sovereignty efforts. This trend reflects how quantum computing success increasingly depends not just on funding scale, but on creating the right ecosystem conditions for interdisciplinary collaboration and talent retention.

Ambition and international talent converge as Denmark scales up in quantum science The post International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology represents a crucial step toward truly scalable neutral atom quantum computers, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits - a scale that could finally deliver practical quantum advantage for real-world problems. Neutral atoms have emerged as one of the most promising qubit platforms because they're naturally identical and can be arranged in flexible geometries, but previous trapping methods have struggled with the massive scalability needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing. By using a single metasurface to generate record numbers of trapped atoms, researchers are addressing one of the key engineering bottlenecks that has limited neutral atom systems to smaller qubit counts. This advance could accelerate the timeline for building quantum computers capable of tackling complex optimization problems, drug discovery, and cryptographic applications that require hundreds of thousands of high-fidelity qubits.

Technique boosts prospects for building quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits The post Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computers must overcome major technical hurdles before tackling quantum chemistry problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This feasibility study provides a much-needed reality check on one of quantum computing's most hyped applications—solving quantum chemistry problems that could revolutionize drug discovery and materials science. While quantum computers are theoretically perfect for simulating quantum systems like molecules, this research reveals that current hardware limitations and algorithmic challenges may delay practical quantum advantage longer than many expect. The comparison of two different algorithmic approaches offers valuable insights for researchers working to bridge the gap between quantum computing's theoretical promise and real-world molecular simulation capabilities. This kind of rigorous analysis is crucial for setting realistic timelines and identifying the specific technical milestones that must be achieved before quantum computers can truly transform chemistry research.

Although the potential applications of quantum computing are widespread, a new feasibility study suggests quantum computers still face major hurdles in solving quantum chemistry problems. The study, published in Physical Review B, evaluates what criteria are needed for a quantum advantage in searching for the ground state energy of molecules. The researchers attempt this feat using two different algorithms with differing strengths and weaknesses.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IonQ | IonQ & University of Cambridge Launch Quantum Innovation Centre
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant leap forward for UK quantum research capabilities, with IonQ's 256-qubit trapped-ion system providing Cambridge researchers access to one of the most advanced quantum computers currently available. The collaboration is particularly noteworthy because it combines IonQ's industry-leading hardware with Cambridge's world-class theoretical quantum research expertise, creating a powerful platform for both fundamental discoveries and practical applications development. This move also signals the growing international competition in quantum technology, as countries race to establish quantum research hubs that can bridge the gap between academic breakthroughs and commercial quantum advantage. The Innovation Centre model could become a template for how quantum hardware companies and universities collaborate to accelerate the field's progress toward practical quantum computing applications.

IonQ partners with the University of Cambridge to deploy a 256-qubit quantum system and launch a new Innovation Centre to accelerate UK quantum research.

Read original at IonQ Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research adds another fascinating entry to the growing catalog of quantum advantages, demonstrating that quantum systems can solve certain permutation parity problems more efficiently than classical computers. What makes this discovery particularly intriguing is that it was "unexpected" - suggesting we're still uncovering new territories where quantum mechanics provides computational benefits beyond the well-known applications like factoring and search algorithms. The work highlights how quantum computing advantages might exist in more mathematical and theoretical domains than previously recognized, potentially opening doors to novel quantum algorithms. While this specific permutation parity task may seem abstract, such fundamental discoveries often serve as building blocks for more practical quantum applications down the road.

The list of things that quantum features enable us to do just got a little longer The post Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in single-photon interferometry over 1.5 km distances represents a significant step toward revolutionizing astronomical observations through quantum-enhanced telescope networks. By using quantum memories to synchronize and correlate photons across widely separated telescopes, astronomers could potentially create virtual telescope arrays with unprecedented resolution - imagine combining telescopes on different continents as if they were a single, massive instrument. The quantum approach could overcome the classical limitations of maintaining coherence over such vast distances, opening possibilities for detecting exoplanets, mapping black hole event horizons, and other observations that require extraordinary precision. While still in early stages, this work bridges quantum information science with one of humanity's oldest scientific pursuits, potentially giving us quantum-powered eyes to see the universe in ways never before possible.

Single-photon interferometry achieved over 1.5 km The post Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant advancement in Majorana qubit readout techniques, which could be crucial for realizing topologically protected quantum computing. Majorana qubits are theoretically more resistant to environmental noise than conventional qubits because their quantum information is stored non-locally, but actually reading out their quantum states has been a major experimental challenge. The development of better readout mechanisms brings us closer to practical topological quantum computers that could operate with much lower error rates, though the researchers acknowledge that scaling these systems to useful sizes remains an open question. This work represents important progress in one of quantum computing's most promising but technically demanding approaches to fault-tolerant computation.

Mechanism could pave the way for more robust quantum computation, but questions remain over scalability The post Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a major leap toward practical quantum-secured communications, as device-independent quantum key distribution over 100 km distances brings us closer to citywide quantum internet infrastructure. The "device-independent" aspect is particularly significant because it eliminates the need to trust the hardware components, making the system inherently more secure against potential manufacturing flaws or malicious tampering. At 100 km range, this technology could realistically connect major institutions, government facilities, and financial centers within metropolitan areas using quantum-encrypted communications that are theoretically unbreakable. This marks a crucial step from laboratory demonstrations to real-world deployment of quantum security networks that could reshape how we protect sensitive data transmission.

Device-independent quantum-encrypted keys distributed over 100 km The post Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Physics World podcast featuring Bert de Jong offers valuable insights into the Quantum Systems Accelerator, a major DOE-funded initiative that's working to bridge the gap between quantum research and practical computing applications. As co-director of this national program, de Jong provides a unique perspective on how government-backed quantum initiatives are tackling some of the most pressing challenges in scaling quantum technologies beyond the lab. The QSA represents one of the largest coordinated efforts to develop the full quantum computing stack, from hardware to software to applications, making this discussion particularly relevant for understanding how policy and funding decisions are shaping the field's trajectory. For anyone tracking the intersection of quantum policy and technology development, this podcast likely contains important updates on federal quantum computing priorities and strategic directions.

Bert de Jong of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab is our podcast guest The post Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating connection between quantum entanglement and computational complexity theory, suggesting that the amount of entanglement required to solve a problem can serve as a measure of its computational difficulty. This research bridges fundamental quantum mechanics with computer science, potentially offering new ways to classify problems and understand why certain calculations are inherently hard even for quantum computers. The findings could help researchers better predict which problems will benefit most from quantum advantage and guide the development of more efficient quantum algorithms. While the summary is brief, this type of work is crucial for establishing theoretical foundations that will shape how we approach quantum computing challenges in the coming years.

Efficiency and hardness of a computation problem are revealed The post Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology represents a crucial scaling milestone for neutral atom quantum computing, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits - far beyond current capabilities that typically max out in the hundreds. Neutral atom platforms have emerged as strong contenders in the quantum computing race due to their flexibility and relatively low error rates, but scaling up the number of precisely controlled atoms has been a major engineering challenge. The ability to create such massive atomic arrays could give neutral atom systems a significant advantage over other quantum computing approaches, particularly for applications requiring large-scale quantum simulations or optimization problems. This development signals that neutral atom quantum computers may soon transition from promising research platforms to practical quantum advantage machines capable of tackling real-world problems.

Technique lays the groundwork for neutral-atom quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits, say physicists The post Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked aspect of quantum technology development: the urgent need for international standards as quantum sensors and metrology devices transition from laboratory curiosities to commercial reality. The collaboration between National Metrology Institutes like NPL represents a critical infrastructure development that will determine whether quantum sensing technologies can achieve their promised precision advantages in real-world applications. Without coordinated international standards, even breakthrough quantum sensors could face adoption barriers similar to early telecommunications technologies, where incompatible systems hindered widespread deployment. This standardization effort is particularly timely as quantum sensors are approaching commercial viability in areas like navigation, medical imaging, and fundamental physics research.

NPL has joined forces with other leading National Metrology Institutes to shape the international standards effort in quantum technologies The post Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone from Silicon Quantum Computing represents a significant step toward making quantum computing more practical and manufacturable, as silicon-based quantum processors could leverage the massive existing infrastructure of semiconductor fabrication. While 11 qubits may seem modest compared to IBM's hundreds of superconducting qubits or trapped ion systems, silicon quantum dots offer unique advantages in terms of scalability and integration with classical electronics that could prove decisive in the long run. The Australian team's achievement demonstrates that silicon spin qubits are maturing as a serious contender in the quantum hardware race, potentially offering a more economical path to fault-tolerant quantum computing. This development underscores how the quantum computing landscape remains wide open, with multiple promising technologies competing to solve the fundamental challenges of building reliable, large-scale quantum processors.

Australian spin-out Silicon Quantum Computing makes the case with a modality-leading 11-qubit processor The post Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers? appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough challenges one of quantum mechanics' most fundamental principles - the no-cloning theorem - by demonstrating that quantum states can indeed be copied when properly encrypted, opening remarkable new possibilities for quantum networking and distributed computing. The ability to store encrypted qubits in multiple locations could revolutionize quantum internet architecture by enabling quantum data to be safely replicated across network nodes, dramatically improving reliability and accessibility of quantum information systems. This elegant workaround to quantum mechanics' copying restrictions represents a significant step toward practical quantum networks that can mirror the redundancy and distributed nature of classical internet infrastructure. While still early-stage research, this development could prove crucial for building robust quantum communication networks that don't lose precious quantum information due to hardware failures or transmission errors.

“Elegant” result has implications for a quantum internet The post Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals Microsoft's continued push to make quantum development more accessible to mainstream programmers, a crucial step toward realizing practical quantum applications. While the summary is brief, enhanced developer tooling typically addresses key pain points like quantum circuit visualization, debugging capabilities, and seamless integration between classical and quantum code - challenges that have historically made quantum programming intimidating for newcomers. Microsoft's Azure Quantum platform has been positioning itself as the "GitHub of quantum computing" by supporting multiple hardware backends and programming languages, so these new tools likely strengthen that ecosystem approach. Developer-friendly tooling is often what transforms experimental technologies into practical platforms, making this the kind of infrastructure investment that could accelerate quantum software development across industries.

Microsoft is committed to providing the tools that developers and scientists need to enable applications on quantum machines. The post Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform appeared first on Microsoft Azure Quantum Blog .

Read original at Microsoft Quantum Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating theoretical framework that treats quantum measurement not as an instantaneous collapse but as a continuous flowing process, offering new insights into one of quantum mechanics' most puzzling aspects. By modeling wavefunction collapse through quantum state diffusion and connecting it to entropy production, researchers are developing a more nuanced understanding of how quantum information behaves during measurement - a fundamental process that underlies all quantum sensing and computing applications. This work could prove particularly valuable for quantum sensing technologies, where understanding the precise dynamics of measurement processes is crucial for optimizing detector sensitivity and minimizing decoherence effects. While still theoretical, this approach represents an intriguing step toward demystifying the measurement problem that has puzzled physicists since the early days of quantum mechanics.

Quantum state diffusion framework makes it possible to characterize quantum measurement in terms of entropy production The post Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
